BTE insurance can fund legal costs without a success fee deduction from damages, making it preferable for clients who have it. Failing to check for BTE insurance before entering a CFA may be a breach of the duty to advise. Option D is wrong — personal injury claims are generally outside the scope of legal aid post-LASPO.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Crane & Pelletier wishes to terminate its retainer with a client who has become abusive and refuses to provide instructions. What must the firm do before terminating?

options.A: Give the client reasonable notice, explain the reason if possible without prejudice to the firm's interests, return the client's papers and property promptly, and not terminate at a time or in circumstances where the client would be prejudiced (SRA Code para 1.2 and para 3.7).
options.B: Simply stop acting — no formalities are required.
options.C: Obtain court permission before ceasing to act.
options.D: Only terminate if the client has been given three written warnings.

Legal Services.explanation: A solicitor has the right to terminate a retainer in appropriate circumstances (such as the client being abusive or failing to give instructions), but must do so responsibly. SRA Code para 3.7 requires that where a solicitor ends a client relationship, they do so in a way that does not harm the client's interests, give reasonable notice, and return documents and property. If the client is in an urgent matter, the timing of termination is particularly important. The firm may retain a lien over documents if unpaid fees are owed (in certain circumstances). Options A, C and D are incorrect.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: The SRA abolished mandatory CPD hours for solicitors in 2016. What replaced the old CPD regime?

options.A: A continuing competence framework — solicitors are expected to reflect on their learning needs, address those needs through appropriate activities, and keep a record of this reflection and learning; the SRA may ask for evidence of competence.
options.B: Nothing — solicitors have no continuing training obligations.
options.C: A requirement to complete 16 CPD hours per year.
options.D: A three-yearly reaccreditation examination.

Legal Services.explanation: In 2016, the SRA replaced the mandatory CPD hours requirement with a framework based on continuing competence. Solicitors must: (i) consider their practice and reflect on what they need to learn; (ii) address those needs through appropriate training and learning activities; and (iii) keep a record of this process. The SRA may require evidence of how a solicitor maintains their competence. This is a more flexible, outcomes-focused approach than prescriptive hourly requirements. Option B is wrong — there are continuing obligations. Options C and D describe the old or a non-existent regime.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Ravi Sharma realises he made a significant error in a matter that has caused the client financial loss. He corrects the error by paying for a remedy from his own funds and does not tell the client or the firm. Is this approach permissible?

options.A: No — SRA Code para 7.10 requires solicitors to report to the SRA promptly if they become aware that there has been a serious breach; separately, clients must be informed of errors that affect them (SRA Code para 1.4 — honesty; para 7.11). Paying privately to cover up the error does not discharge these obligations.
options.B: Yes — he remedied the situation so there is nothing to report.
options.C: Yes — provided the client is not aware of the error.
options.D: Yes — private remediation is encouraged as it avoids unnecessary complaints.

Legal Services.explanation: SRA Code para 7.10 requires solicitors to ensure they report promptly to the SRA anything material they reasonably believe they ought to report. Para 1.4 requires honesty and transparency. Clients must be told of material errors (para 7.11 and the duty of candour). Covertly remedying an error — however well-intentioned — without informing the client or firm prevents the client from seeking independent advice about what has happened and prevents proper oversight of the error. Depending on the seriousness, the firm's insurer may also need to be notified. Options A, C and D all condone concealment, which the SRA Code does not permit.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Crane & Pelletier holds £8,000 of Northgate Couriers Ltd's money in the client account. The firm has delivered a bill for £3,000 and wishes to transfer that amount to the office account. What must the firm ensure before making this transfer?

options.A: The transfer is permissible under the SRA Accounts Rules 2019: money can be transferred from the client account to the office account in payment of costs once a bill has been delivered; the firm need not wait for the client to pay separately.
options.B: The client has paid the bill in full from a separate source.
options.C: The SRA has authorised the specific withdrawal.
options.D: The client has signed a written authority for each individual transfer.

Legal Services.explanation: SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 5 permits withdrawal from a client account to pay the firm's costs where a bill of costs or other written notification of costs has been issued to the client. Once a bill is delivered, the firm may transfer the billed amount from the client account to the office account without waiting for a separate payment. No SRA authorisation (C) or individual written authority (D) is needed for routine costs transfers following a delivered bill. Option B would leave the client paying twice for the same costs.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: After a matter is concluded, Crane & Pelletier holds a residual balance of £150 of client money that cannot be returned to Hassan Nazari (whose address is unknown despite reasonable attempts to trace him). What may the firm do?

options.A: Retain the money in client account indefinitely.
options.B: Transfer the money to the office account immediately without any formalities.
options.C: After making reasonable attempts to return the money (and keeping a record), the firm may pay the money to a charity of its choice, provided it undertakes to replace it if the client subsequently claims it (SRA Accounts Rules 2019 rule 5.1(c) and SRA guidance).
options.D: Report the money as abandoned to the Treasury Solicitor.

Legal Services.explanation: The SRA Accounts Rules 2019 and accompanying guidance address residual client balances. Where the firm cannot return client money despite reasonable efforts, it may (after documenting those efforts) pay the balance to a charity and give an undertaking to repay if the client later claims the money. The firm must keep adequate records. Simply retaining the money (A) or taking it to office account (B) without proper process would breach the rules. Reporting to the Treasury Solicitor (D) is not the prescribed mechanism under the SRA framework.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: The SRA Transparency Rules require solicitors' firms to publish certain information on their websites. Which of the following is a required publication?

options.A: A list of all clients the firm has ever acted for.
options.B: The personal financial details of all partners.
options.C: Pricing information for specified services (including residential conveyancing, employment tribunal claims, motoring offences, and debt recovery), including the basis for charging and the range of likely total costs.
options.D: The firm's full client account bank statements.

Legal Services.explanation: The SRA Transparency Rules (2018, updated) require firms and individuals providing certain reserved legal activities to publish specified information on their websites. For the specified services (residential conveyancing, employment tribunals, motoring offences, immigration, debt recovery, probate, and mental health tribunals), firms must publish: the total cost or range of costs; the basis of charging; the experience of those who will work on the matter; and a description of the key stages. This is intended to help consumers make informed choices. Options A, C and D are not required publications.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Ravi Sharma is a solicitor employed by Northgate Couriers Ltd as an in-house lawyer. He advises only his employer and no external clients. Which SRA regulatory obligations apply to him?

options.A: None — in-house solicitors are not regulated by the SRA.
options.B: Only the Solicitors Act 1974 applies — not the SRA Code.
options.C: In-house solicitors are individually regulated by the SRA and must comply with the SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors; however, the SRA Code for Firms does not apply to them unless they are also a manager of an SRA-authorised firm.
options.D: In-house solicitors must comply with the Code of Conduct for Firms only.

Legal Services.explanation: Solicitors practising as in-house lawyers are individually regulated by the SRA and must comply with the SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors (the individual code). The SRA Code of Conduct for Firms (the firm code) applies to authorised bodies — an employer company that is not itself an SRA-authorised firm is not subject to the firm code, but the individual solicitor remains subject to the individual code. Option A is wrong — in-house solicitors are regulated by the SRA. Option B understates the regulatory framework. Option D applies the wrong code.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Crane & Pelletier acted for James Ashworth in a business dispute three years ago. It now wishes to act for Victoria Ashworth (James's business partner) against James in a new dispute. James's confidential information from the old matter is relevant to the new claim. Can the firm act?

options.A: Yes — three years is long enough for the old retainer to be irrelevant.
options.B: Yes — provided James gives written consent.
options.C: No — the firm holds confidential information about James that is material to the new matter against him; it cannot act for Victoria without breaching James's confidentiality, and the conflict and confidentiality rules (SRA Code paras 6.3 and 6.4) prevent it from doing so.
options.D: Yes — provided an information barrier is put in place between the teams.

Legal Services.explanation: SRA Code para 6.3 imposes an ongoing duty of confidentiality to former clients. Para 6.4 provides that where a firm holds confidential information about one person that is material to another's matter, and it cannot be disclosed without consent, the firm must not act in circumstances where it cannot properly serve the second client without using that information. With confidential information about James that is relevant to the claim against him, the firm cannot act for Victoria without breaching James's confidentiality. Information barriers (D) may be permissible in some circumstances but require very careful analysis and are unlikely to be sufficient here given the direct relevance. James's consent (C) would be needed — but the question states no consent.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Which of the following correctly describes the role of the Compliance Officer for Finance and Administration (COFA) at an SRA-authorised firm?

options.A: The COFA manages all client-facing work and supervises fee earners.
options.B: The COFA is the same as the COLP.
options.C: The COFA is responsible for the firm's compliance with the SRA Accounts Rules and other financial regulatory obligations, including monitoring client account operations and reporting breaches to the SRA.
options.D: The COFA role is optional for small firms.

Legal Services.explanation: Every SRA-authorised firm must have a COFA (and a COLP). The COFA is responsible for: (i) ensuring the firm complies with the SRA Accounts Rules; (ii) keeping a record of any facts or matters that may indicate a breach; (iii) reporting such matters to the SRA promptly. The COFA is distinct from the COLP, who is responsible for broader legal practice compliance (A and C are wrong). Both roles are mandatory for all authorised firms, not just larger ones (D is wrong).

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Gerald Crane is instructed by a client based in a high-risk third country (as designated by the UK government) on a significant commercial property transaction. Standard CDD has been carried out. What additional obligations arise?

options.A: No additional obligations — standard CDD is always sufficient.
options.B: The transaction must be declined if the client is from a high-risk country.
options.C: Enhanced due diligence (EDD) is required under the Money Laundering Regulations 2017 reg.33 for business relationships or transactions involving persons established in high-risk third countries; this includes obtaining additional information about the client, source of funds and wealth, and senior management approval for the relationship.
options.D: Enhanced CDD is only required for transactions over £1 million.

Legal Services.explanation: Money Laundering Regulations 2017 reg.33 requires regulated firms to apply enhanced due diligence where there is a higher risk of money laundering, including where the customer or transaction involves a high-risk third country as designated by HM Treasury. EDD measures include: obtaining additional information on the customer and beneficial owner; verifying source of funds and wealth; obtaining senior management approval before establishing the relationship; and increased monitoring. The transaction need not be declined simply because of geographic risk (C). There is no monetary threshold for EDD (D). Standard CDD alone is insufficient (A).

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Diana Pelletier has filed a Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) with the National Crime Agency about a client transaction. The client calls to ask why the transaction has been delayed. What must Pelletier not do?

options.A: Tell the client the transaction is under review for routine compliance.
options.B: Ask the NCA for permission to proceed with the transaction.
options.C: Disclose to the client that a SAR has been filed or that a money laundering investigation is underway — this would be the offence of tipping off under Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 s.333A.
options.D: Carry out the transaction while waiting for consent from the NCA.

Legal Services.explanation: POCA 2002 s.333A makes it an offence to disclose to any person that a SAR has been made or that a money laundering investigation is being considered or carried out, if the disclosure is likely to prejudice any investigation. This is 'tipping off'. Pelletier must not tell the client about the SAR. She can explain there is a delay without giving the reason, or seek legal advice about what she can say. Option A (saying it's routine compliance) may or may not be tipping off depending on context — extreme care is needed. Option B describes the 'consent' route under POCA — seeking a 'no objection' from the NCA before proceeding. Option D — proceeding without consent while a SAR is pending could be a principal money laundering offence.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: The SRA intervenes in a law firm following evidence of serious financial irregularities. What is the effect of an SRA intervention?

options.A: The firm is given 30 days to rectify its practices.
options.B: The intervention automatically suspends all solicitors in the firm.
options.C: On intervention, the SRA takes control of the firm's practice; all client files and money in client accounts vest in the SRA; the firm ceases to act for clients; and the SRA arranges for the protection of clients and return of files and money.
options.D: The firm is wound up by the court.

Legal Services.explanation: Under the Solicitors Act 1974 Schedule 1, when the SRA intervenes in a practice, it takes control of the firm. The key effects include: the firm's right to practise ceases; all sums of client money vest in the SRA; all documents and records relating to the practice vest in the SRA; and the SRA arranges for clients to receive their papers and for the safeguarding of client money. Individual solicitors are not automatically suspended by the intervention itself (C) — separate suspension or striking-off proceedings may follow. Winding up (D) is a separate company law/insolvency process.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Ravi Sharma, in a witness statement, refers to and quotes extensively from a privileged legal advice memo. Has privilege been waived?

options.A: No — referring to a document in a witness statement never waives privilege.
options.B: No — privilege can only be waived in writing signed by the client.
options.C: Yes — where a party refers to and quotes from a privileged document in court proceedings, privilege will generally be taken to have been waived in respect of that document; the other party may seek to see the whole document.
options.D: Yes — but only if the quote is more than 50 words long.

Legal Services.explanation: Privilege can be waived expressly or by conduct. Referring to the contents of a privileged document in court proceedings (particularly where the contents are relied upon as evidence) amounts to a waiver of privilege in respect of that document — the court will not allow a party to selectively deploy part of a document while maintaining privilege over the rest. The principle of collateral waiver means that if privilege is waived for part of a document or advice, it may be waived for the whole. Options A and C are wrong. Option D invents a word-count threshold.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: A litigation funder agrees to finance Northgate Couriers Ltd's commercial claim against a supplier in return for 30% of any damages recovered. Which statement best describes the regulatory position of third party litigation funding in England and Wales?

options.A: Third party litigation funding is prohibited as champertous.
options.B: Third party funders must be authorised by the FCA.
options.C: Third party litigation funding by commercial funders is lawful in England and Wales following the abolition of the torts of maintenance and champerty for most purposes by the Criminal Law Act 1967; it is regulated primarily by the Association of Litigation Funders' Code of Conduct rather than by statute.
options.D: Litigation funding agreements are always unlawful if they involve sharing damages.

Legal Services.explanation: The Criminal Law Act 1967 abolished the crimes and torts of maintenance and champerty, making third party litigation funding lawful for most purposes. Commercial litigation funding — where a funder finances a claim in return for a share of proceeds — is now an established market in England and Wales. The sector is self-regulated by the Association of Litigation Funders (ALF) Code of Conduct rather than by statutory regulation. Courts scrutinise funding agreements to ensure they do not improperly interfere with litigation or undermine access to justice. FCA authorisation (C) is not required for pure litigation funders. Options A and D reflect the pre-1967 law.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: The SDT hears an allegation of dishonesty against Diana Pelletier. What standard of proof applies?

options.A: Balance of probabilities — because this is a civil regulatory tribunal.
options.B: The SDT applies its own bespoke 'clear and convincing evidence' standard.
options.C: Beyond reasonable doubt — dishonesty allegations against solicitors are proved to the criminal standard at the SDT (Solicitors Act 1974 s.46; confirmed in Solicitors Regulation Authority v Sharma [2010]).
options.D: No standard of proof applies — the SDT decides on the merits.

Legal Services.explanation: The SDT applies the criminal standard of proof (beyond reasonable doubt) to allegations of dishonesty. This was confirmed in SRA v Sharma [2010] EWHC 2022 (Admin). For other allegations not involving dishonesty, the civil standard (balance of probabilities) applies. The heightened standard for dishonesty reflects the gravity of such findings and their potential to destroy a professional career. Option A applies the wrong standard for dishonesty. Option B is not an English law standard. Option D is wrong.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Ravi Sharma is instructed by an elderly client, Colonel Ashworth, who appears confused during the meeting. He struggles to recall recent events and requires explanation. What additional steps should Sharma take?

options.A: Proceed as normal — age alone does not affect a client's legal capacity.
options.B: Refuse to act until the client's GP confirms capacity.
options.C: Consider whether Colonel Ashworth has the mental capacity to give valid instructions; if capacity is in doubt, take steps to assess and support capacity (referring to the Mental Capacity Act 2005 principles); consider whether a third party is exerting undue influence; document all steps taken.
options.D: Take instructions from the client's next of kin instead.

Legal Services.explanation: SRA Principle 7 requires solicitors to act in the best interests of each client, which includes recognising when a client may have diminished capacity or be vulnerable. The Mental Capacity Act 2005 s.1 provides that capacity is presumed, but if there are reasonable grounds to doubt it, steps must be taken to assess it. Solicitors should: take time to explain matters clearly; assess whether the client understands the nature and consequences of the instructions; be alert to signs of undue influence by third parties; and document their approach. Refusing to act unless a GP confirms capacity (C) is unnecessary if the solicitor can make a reasonable assessment. Taking instructions from next of kin (D) would be inappropriate unless a lasting power of attorney is in place.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.subtopic: Solicitor and client costs — Solicitors Act 1974 s.70
Legal Services.stem: A client disputes a bill delivered by Crane & Pelletier. The client wishes to have the bill assessed by the court. What right does the client have?

options.A: The client has no right to challenge a delivered bill.
options.B: The client can only challenge the bill if the amount exceeds £10,000.
options.C: The client may apply for an assessment of the solicitor's bill under Solicitors Act 1974 s.70 (now CPR Part 46, s.II); the application must generally be made within one month of delivery of the bill (as of right) or within 12 months (at the court's discretion).
options.D: The client must first complain to the Legal Ombudsman before applying for assessment.

Legal Services.explanation: Solicitors Act 1974 s.70 gives a client the right to apply to the court for assessment (formerly 'taxation') of a solicitor's bill. As of right, the client can apply within one month of delivery of the bill; the court has discretion to order assessment after one month but within 12 months. After 12 months, the court will only order assessment in special circumstances. There is no monetary threshold (C). The Legal Ombudsman route (D) is separate — the client may choose either or both routes. Assessment is conducted on the solicitor-and-client basis.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Which of the following is a reserved legal activity under the Legal Services Act 2007 that can only be carried out by an authorised person?

options.A: Providing general legal advice.
options.B: Drafting standard commercial contracts.
options.C: The exercise of a right of audience, the conduct of litigation, conveyancing, probate activities, notarial activities, and the administration of oaths.
options.D: Carrying out legal research.

Legal Services.explanation: Legal Services Act 2007 s.12 and Schedule 2 define six reserved legal activities: (1) exercise of a right of audience; (2) conduct of litigation; (3) reserved instrument activities (conveyancing); (4) probate activities; (5) notarial activities; (6) administration of oaths. These can only be carried out by 'authorised persons' (e.g., solicitors, barristers, legal executives, licensed conveyancers) or exempt persons. General legal advice (A), drafting contracts (C), and legal research (D) are not reserved — anyone can provide these services, though only authorised persons can use protected titles (e.g., 'solicitor').

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: A paralegal working at Crane & Pelletier describes herself in correspondence as a 'solicitor' when she is not qualified. What is the consequence?

options.A: This is permitted if the firm's supervising solicitor approves.
options.B: The paralegal must simply clarify her status in the next correspondence.
options.C: It is only an issue if the client complains.
options.D: Using the title 'solicitor' when not admitted as one is a criminal offence under the Solicitors Act 1974 s.21; it is also a breach of professional standards.

Legal Services.explanation: Solicitors Act 1974 s.21 makes it a criminal offence for an unqualified person to wilfully pretend to be, or take or use any name, title, addition or description implying that he or she is, qualified or recognised by law as qualified to act as a solicitor. 'Solicitor' is a protected title that may only be used by persons admitted to the roll and holding a current practising certificate. This is a serious matter regardless of client complaint (C). Supervisor approval (A) cannot cure the criminal offence. A single clarifying letter (D) does not undo the earlier misrepresentation.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.subtopic: Lender and borrower — mortgage fraud risk
Legal Services.stem: Crane & Pelletier acts for both the buyer and the lender in a residential purchase. During the transaction, the solicitor discovers the buyer has provided the lender with false information about the purchase price (there is a secret cash-back arrangement). What must the firm do?

options.A: Continue acting and keep both clients informed.
options.B: Do nothing — the solicitor cannot be held responsible for the client's misrepresentation.
options.C: Cease acting for the buyer only and continue for the lender.
options.D: Inform the lender immediately of the discrepancy, even if this means breaching the buyer's confidentiality — the CML Lenders' Handbook obligation to report material information to the lender overrides confidentiality in this context; alternatively, cease acting for both.

Legal Services.explanation: When acting for both buyer and lender under the Council of Mortgage Lenders (CML, now UK Finance) Lenders' Handbook, the solicitor undertakes to report to the lender anything that may affect the lender's decision to lend. A secret cash-back arrangement that misrepresents the true purchase price is a material fact that must be reported to the lender. In this situation, the duty to the lender overrides the buyer's confidentiality (because the solicitor undertook, in accepting the joint instruction, to report such matters). If the buyer refuses consent to disclosure, the firm must cease acting for both parties. Option A continues to hide material information from the lender. Option C — the firm may not be able to continue for the lender either if doing so would use confidential buyer information. Option B ignores the reporting obligation.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Which of the following is one of the regulatory objectives set out in the Legal Services Act 2007 s.1?

options.A: Maximising solicitors' earnings.
options.B: Preventing any non-lawyer involvement in legal services.
options.C: Ensuring the highest possible legal fees are charged.
options.D: Protecting and promoting the public interest.

Legal Services.explanation: Legal Services Act 2007 s.1 sets out the regulatory objectives: (a) protecting and promoting the public interest; (b) supporting the constitutional principle of the rule of law; (c) improving access to justice; (d) protecting and promoting the interests of consumers; (e) promoting competition in the provision of services; (f) encouraging an independent, strong, diverse and effective legal profession; (g) increasing public understanding of citizens' legal rights and duties; (h) promoting and maintaining adherence to professional principles. Options A and C are contrary to the objectives. Option B is contrary to the ABS framework.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Crane & Pelletier is instructed by Victoria Ashworth to act in a straightforward boundary dispute. During the matter, Victoria asks the firm to also advise her on an unrelated employment dispute. Is the firm obliged to advise on the employment matter?

options.A: Yes — solicitors must advise on any legal matter a client raises.
options.B: No — the firm must refer Victoria to an employment specialist without any further involvement.
options.C: Yes — provided the firm has a solicitor qualified in employment law.
options.D: No — the firm's retainer is limited to the scope of instructions agreed at the outset; the employment matter is outside the agreed retainer; the firm may agree to take on the additional matter under a new or extended retainer, but has no obligation to do so.

Legal Services.explanation: A solicitor's duties are defined by the scope of the retainer agreed with the client. If instructed only on the boundary dispute, the firm is not obliged to advise on an unrelated employment matter. However, the firm should consider whether declining creates any risk for the client (e.g., an urgent employment matter with time limits) and act in the client's best interests accordingly — which may include advising the client to seek specialist employment advice or offering to extend the retainer. Option A overstates the obligation. Option B is unnecessarily rigid — the firm may agree to help.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.subtopic: Insured v uninsured position — run-off cover
Legal Services.stem: Crane & Pelletier closes down. A former client subsequently brings a negligence claim in respect of work done before closure. What insurance protection should have been in place?

options.A: No insurance is required after a firm closes.
options.B: The SRA Compensation Fund covers all post-closure claims automatically.
options.C: The former partners are personally liable with no insurance protection.
options.D: Qualifying run-off cover — the SRA's minimum terms require that on closure a firm arranges qualifying run-off cover for at least six years to protect former clients who bring claims after the firm has closed.

Legal Services.explanation: The SRA Indemnity Insurance Rules require that on cessation of a practice, the former firm (or its former principals) must arrange qualifying run-off cover for a minimum of six years following closure. This ensures former clients who suffer loss as a result of the firm's work (and who only discover the problem after closure) can still make a claim against a funded insurer. The SRA Compensation Fund (D) provides a safety net of last resort but does not replace the run-off insurance obligation. Option A and C ignore the run-off requirement.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: The SRA Compensation Fund makes a payment to a client who suffered loss due to their solicitor's dishonesty. What contribution does the client need to make, and is this a right or a discretion?

options.A: The client must contribute 50% of the loss.
options.B: The client must exhaust all civil claims before applying to the Fund.
options.C: The Fund automatically pays all losses without any assessment.
options.D: Payment from the Compensation Fund is discretionary — the SRA has a discretion whether to make a grant; the client need not contribute to losses; grants can be made where a client suffers loss due to a solicitor's dishonesty or failure to account, subject to the SRA's rules and maximum grant limits.

Legal Services.explanation: The SRA Compensation Fund is a fund of last resort maintained by the SRA (funded by annual contributions from solicitors). Grants from the Fund are discretionary — the SRA has a discretion whether to make a grant in any given case and subject to what conditions. There is no requirement for the client to contribute to their own loss. However, the SRA may take into account whether the applicant has taken reasonable steps to recover the loss through other means. There are maximum grant limits set by the SRA. Option A is wrong. Option C is wrong — there is no automatic right. Option B would be too onerous and is not required.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.subtopic: Disclosure in the public interest — serious crime
Legal Services.stem: Diana Pelletier discovers in the course of acting for a client that the client plans to commit a serious violent crime against a named individual. The client refuses to abandon the plan. What may Pelletier do?

options.A: Nothing — the duty of confidentiality is absolute.
options.B: She can only disclose if the court orders her to do so.
options.C: She must disclose immediately to the police without any further consideration.
options.D: Pelletier may disclose information in exceptional circumstances involving a risk of serious harm to an identified individual, but only if the public interest in disclosure clearly outweighs the duty of confidentiality.

Legal Services.explanation: Confidentiality is a fundamental duty (SRA Code para 6.3) but is not absolute. Where a client reveals a clear intention to commit a serious crime that will cause serious harm to an identified person, the solicitor may (but is not generally required to) disclose to prevent the crime. This exception is narrow: the risk must be serious, imminent, and identifiable; disclosure should be to the minimum extent necessary. This is a permission, not a duty, and Pelletier should seek advice (e.g., from the SRA ethics helpline) before disclosing. Option A overstates the absoluteness of confidentiality. Option C creates an immediate mandatory duty that does not exist. Option B is wrong — court order is not required for this exception.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: The SRA investigates Ravi Sharma for a regulatory breach. Rather than referring the matter to the SDT, the SRA proposes a regulatory settlement. What does this involve?

options.A: A criminal prosecution by the SRA.
options.B: Automatic striking-off from the roll.
options.C: A private warning with no formal record.
options.D: A Regulatory Settlement Agreement (RSA) is a formal resolution between the SRA and the regulated person, agreed without a full SDT hearing; the solicitor admits the breach and agrees to sanctions (which may include a fine, rebuke, or conditions); this avoids the cost and uncertainty of SDT proceedings.

Legal Services.explanation: Regulatory Settlement Agreements allow the SRA and the regulated person to agree a formal resolution to a disciplinary matter without a full SDT hearing. The solicitor admits the breach and agrees to specific outcomes (e.g., fine, rebuke, conditions on practising certificate). RSAs are published on the SRA's website. They are appropriate for cases that warrant formal sanction but do not require the SDT's more serious powers (suspension or striking-off). Options A, C and D do not describe RSAs.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: A client dismisses Crane & Pelletier mid-matter and refuses to pay outstanding fees of £5,000. The firm wishes to retain the client's papers until payment. Is this permissible?

options.A: No — a solicitor cannot withhold client papers under any circumstances.
options.B: No — the client's papers must be returned immediately and fees pursued separately.
options.C: Yes — but only for 14 days after the client requests the papers.
options.D: Yes — a solicitor has a common law lien over a client's papers and documents for unpaid fees; the firm may retain the papers until the fees are paid, provided retention does not cause injustice to the client.

Legal Services.explanation: At common law, a solicitor has a possessory (or retaining) lien over a client's papers, documents, and funds in the solicitor's possession, as security for unpaid fees. The solicitor may retain the papers until the fees are paid. However, this right is not absolute — courts can intervene where exercise of the lien would cause injustice (e.g., where the client faces an urgent legal deadline and the papers are essential). The SRA Code also requires that the exercise of any lien does not harm the client's interests unduly. Option A is wrong. Option C introduces a time limit that does not exist. Option B is wrong.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Under the SRA's framework, what obligation do SRA-authorised firms have regarding diversity data?

options.A: Firms must collect and publish workforce diversity data annually to the SRA.
options.B: Only large firms (50+ staff) must collect diversity data.
options.C: Diversity data collection is voluntary.
options.D: Firms must collect workforce diversity data from staff and managers, report it to the SRA on a biennial basis, and publish a summary of the data (subject to exceptions where disclosure would identify individuals).

Legal Services.explanation: The SRA requires authorised firms to collect diversity data from their workforce (staff and managers) and to report it to the SRA. This data is used to track progress on diversity and inclusion across the profession. Firms must also publish a summary of their diversity data on their website (subject to the exception where publication would identify individuals). The requirement is biennial (every two years). All SRA-authorised firms are subject to this requirement, not just large ones (D). It is not merely voluntary (C). The reporting cycle is biennial, not annual (A).

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.subtopic: Duty to advise on alternative routes
Legal Services.stem: Gerald Crane is advising Clive Dring on a dispute with a contractor about defective building work worth £8,000. Crane proposes issuing County Court proceedings. What other option should Crane proactively consider advising Dring about?

options.A: Nothing further — proceedings are always the best option.
options.B: Reporting the contractor to the police.
options.C: Arbitration in the Supreme Court.
options.D: Small claims mediation and the small claims track (which is designed for claims up to £10,000 and where legal costs are not generally recoverable) — Crane should advise that this is a proportionate, low-cost route that may be more appropriate for an £8,000 dispute, consistent with the duty to act in the client's best interests.

Legal Services.explanation: SRA Principle 7 requires solicitors to act in the best interests of each client, which includes advising on the most appropriate and cost-effective route for the client's dispute. For a claim of £8,000, the small claims track is the appropriate track (claims up to £10,000), and costs are not generally recoverable in small claims — meaning the client bears their own legal costs win or lose. A solicitor's charges for pursuing a small claim may therefore be disproportionate. Crane should advise Dring about small claims mediation and the small claims track as potentially more proportionate alternatives. Option A does not serve the client's best interests. Options C and D are inappropriate.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: A property developer hires a non-qualified assistant to prepare and submit Land Registry applications for transfers of freehold property. Is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — Land Registry applications are administrative tasks anyone can carry out.
options.B: No — only barristers can carry out conveyancing work.
options.C: Yes — provided the developer supervises the assistant.
options.D: No — the preparation of transfers of freehold land for consideration is a 'reserved instrument activity' under the Legal Services Act 2007; only authorised persons (e.g., solicitors, licensed conveyancers) may carry this out.

Legal Services.explanation: Legal Services Act 2007 Schedule 2 defines 'reserved instrument activities' to include preparing any instrument of transfer or charge for the purposes of the Land Registration Act 2002. This is a reserved legal activity that can only be carried out by an authorised person (e.g., solicitor, licensed conveyancer, notary, legal executive with appropriate authorisation). A non-qualified person carrying out this activity commits an offence. Supervision by the developer (C) does not cure the lack of authorisation. Barristers (D) also have relevant authorisation but are not the only authorised persons.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.subtopic: Obligation to co-operate with the SRA
Legal Services.stem: The SRA requests that Diana Pelletier produce files relating to a matter under investigation. Pelletier believes the request is disproportionate and declines to produce the files. Which SRA Code obligation does Pelletier risk breaching?

options.A: No obligation — solicitors can decline disproportionate SRA requests.
options.B: Pelletier can decline but must give written reasons within 30 days.
options.C: Only para 1.4 (integrity) is relevant.
options.D: SRA Code para 7.3 requires solicitors to co-operate with the SRA's investigations and audits; failure to produce documents when requested (without legitimate legal justification such as privilege) is a breach that can itself become the subject of disciplinary proceedings.

Legal Services.explanation: SRA Code para 7.3 requires solicitors to respond promptly to the SRA and co-operate with any audit, investigation, or other regulatory process. Declining to produce files requested in an investigation (without a valid legal basis such as privilege) is itself a breach of the Code and can result in separate disciplinary proceedings. Proportionality concerns should be raised with the SRA, but unilateral non-compliance is not permissible. Options A and D permit non-compliance without proper basis. Option C is incomplete.

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Priya Mehta wishes to bring a personal injury claim under a CFA. Her solicitor recommends she also take out After the Event (ATE) insurance. What is the purpose of ATE insurance?

options.A: To fund the solicitor's success fee.
options.B: ATE insurance covers only the claimant's own legal fees.
options.C: ATE insurance is mandatory for all personal injury claims.
options.D: ATE insurance protects the claimant against liability for the opponent's costs and disbursements (such as court fees and expert fees) if the claim is lost; under post-LASPO rules, the ATE premium is not recoverable from the defendant if the claim succeeds.

Legal Services.explanation: After the Event insurance is purchased after the event giving rise to the claim (hence the name) and protects the claimant against adverse costs orders (paying the defendant's costs if the claim fails) and against the claimant's own disbursements (court fees, expert fees). Under LASPO 2012, ATE premiums are no longer recoverable from the losing defendant in most cases — the claimant bears the cost. ATE insurance does not fund the success fee (A — that comes out of damages under post-LASPO rules). It is not mandatory (C). It covers costs liabilities, not the claimant's own legal fees per se (D).

Legal Services.subject: Legal Services
Legal Services.stem: Gerald Crane instructs a barrister to act in a criminal case. The barrister refuses on the basis that she finds the client's alleged conduct morally repugnant. Is this refusal permissible?

options.A: Yes — barristers have a right to decline any instruction on any ground.
options.B: No — but only if the barrister is publicly funded.
options.C: Yes — moral objections always constitute a valid exception to the cab-rank rule.
options.D: No — the cab-rank rule in the BSB Handbook (rC29) requires a barrister to accept any brief or instructions in a field in which she holds herself out as practising, at a proper fee, unless one of the specific exceptions applies; personal distaste for the nature of the case is not a permitted ground for refusal.

Legal Services.explanation: The cab-rank rule (BSB Handbook rC29) is a fundamental principle of the Bar requiring a self-employed barrister to accept any brief or instructions in a field in which they hold themselves out as practising, at a proper fee, unless one of the specific exceptions applies (such as: a conflict of interest; insufficient time to prepare; the instructions require the barrister to do something contrary to her professional obligations; she is not competent in the area; or the brief is outside her expertise). Personal distaste or moral objection to the nature of the case is not a permitted exception. This rule underpins access to justice by ensuring unpopular clients can be represented. Solicitors do not have an equivalent cab-rank rule.
